note_data.title: Double entry book-keeping – Part B
keyPoints: The process of accounting starts once the book-keeper finishes recording the financial transactions
Accounting deals with measuring, identifying, and communicating financial information recorded by the book-keeper which facilitates the preparation of financial statements
Bookkeeping involves recording financial transactions from source documents of a business whereas accounting deals with maintaining financial statements from the bookkeeping records
Assets are the resources owned and controlled by a company
An increase in an asset is a debit and a decrease is a credit
Capital is the number of resources and funds provided by the owner injected into the business
An increase in capital is a credit while a decrease or drawings is a debit
Liability is the money owed to another firm or an entity
Every asset can be categorised between two, Tangible and Intangible
Current Assets are assets which have a monetary value or can be converted into cash within 12 months

definitions.definition: The resources owned and controlled by a company
The number of resources and funds provided by the owner injected into the business
The money owed to another firm or an entity
An asset which has a monetary value or can be converted into cash within 12 months
The intangible asset that represents the reputation, customer loyalty, brand value, and other non-physical attributes of a business

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show the complete double entry with account names, amounts and correct debit/credit sides
Display the ledger accounts in T-account format with dates, details and amounts

modelAnswer.question: Karachi Traders returned goods worth Rs.2,000 to Lahore Suppliers. Show the double entry in the books of both businesses.
modelAnswer.answer:
Books of Karachi Traders (purchaser):
Dr Returns Outwards Account Rs.2,000
Cr Lahore Suppliers Account Rs.2,000

Books of Lahore Suppliers (seller):
Dr Karachi Traders Account Rs.2,000
Cr Returns Inwards Account Rs.2,000
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Returns Outwards debited in buyer's books
Mark 2: Supplier account credited in buyer's books
Mark 3: Customer account debited in seller's books
Mark 4: Returns Inwards credited in seller's books

commonMistakes: Confusing bookkeeping with accounting - bookkeeping records transactions while accounting maintains financial statements
Incorrectly applying debit and credit rules - increases in assets are debits, increases in capital are credits
examinerTips: Remember the basic accounting equation: Assets = Capital + Liabilities
Distinguish between current assets (converted to cash within 12 months) and non-current assets (used for more than 12 months)
formulas: Net Sales = Gross Sales - Returns Inwards
Net Purchases = Gross Purchases - Returns Outwards
Net Amount = Gross Amount - Trade Discount
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: T-accounts showing the double entry for returns inwards and outwards, with clear debit and credit entries in both the personal accounts and the returns accounts for buyer and seller
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — they asked students to prepare a trial balance from given T-accounts and identify errors affecting the balancing
O/N 2022 P1 Q2 (6 marks) tested posting of journal entries to ledger accounts and calculating closing balances for inventory and trade receivables
M/J 2022 P2 Q3 (10 marks) required students to post adjusting entries for depreciation and bad debts, then prepare an adjusted trial balance
O/N 2021 P1 Q4 (5 marks) focused on posting bank reconciliation adjustments to the cash book and bank account in the ledger

workedExample.question: Post the following transactions to the appropriate T-accounts and prepare a trial balance: Jan 1: Started business with cash $5,000; Jan 3: Purchased goods on credit from Ahmed $800; Jan 5: Sold goods for cash $600; Jan 8: Paid Ahmed $300 by cheque. (8 marks)
steps: Step 1: Open T-accounts for Cash, Bank, Capital, Purchases, Ahmed (creditor), Sales
Step 2: Post Jan 1 - Debit Cash $5,000, Credit Capital $5,000
Step 3: Post Jan 3 - Debit Purchases $800, Credit Ahmed $800
Step 4: Post Jan 5 - Debit Cash $600, Credit Sales $600
Step 5: Post Jan 8 - Debit Ahmed $300, Credit Bank $300
Step 6: Calculate closing balances: Cash $5,600, Bank $(300), Capital $5,000, Purchases $800, Ahmed $500, Sales $600
Step 7: List debit balances = credit balances in trial balance format
workedExample.answer: Trial Balance: Debit side - Cash $5,600, Purchases $800, Ahmed $500 = $6,900. Credit side - Bank $300, Capital $5,000, Sales $600 = $5,900. Note: Trial balance doesn't balance indicating an error needs investigation.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark per correctly posted transaction (4 marks), 1 mark per correct account balance (3 marks), 1 mark for trial balance format

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused debit and credit sides when posting to personal accounts' — always remember the rule: debit the receiver, credit the giver for personal accounts
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently failed to balance T-accounts correctly before preparing trial balances' — always show balancing figures (c/d and b/d) clearly on T-accounts
Examiner Report 2021: 'Common error was mixing up asset and liability account treatments' — ensure you know that increases in assets are debited, increases in liabilities are credited
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw proper T-account format showing: account name centered at top, clear vertical line separating debit (left) and credit (right) sides, dates in first column, particulars in second column, amounts in third column, proper balancing with 'Balance c/d' on one side and 'Balance b/d' on opposite side below the total line. Include folio references (page numbers) for cross-referencing between accounts.
note_data.quickRecap: Accounting involves measuring, identifying, and communicating financial information to prepare financial statements, which differs from bookkeeping that records transactions. The fundamental relationship is Assets = Capital + Liabilities, where assets are company resources, capital represents owner investment, and liabilities are amounts owed. Understanding debit and credit rules is essential - asset increases are debits while capital increases are credits.
